text.title: Frontline Validation Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that prevent the most common failure of process improvement: an as-is model that captures the official process but misses the real one.

rule.statement: Validate every process variant with ≥ 2 different frontline operators using direct observation. A "variant" is any branch in the documented process (region, customer segment, exception path, shift).
rule.rationale: A single operator's process reflects their personal workaround; two diverge enough to expose where one (or both) deviate from the documented version.

rule.statement: Conduct observation (shadowing or gemba walk) BEFORE the structured listening session with the same operator. Do not interview-then-observe.
rule.rationale: Pre-interview shapes the operator's narrative. Post-observation interviews surface workarounds because the operator can no longer hide what the BA already saw.

rule.statement: Tag every observation note with exactly one of: workaround, deviation, blocker, tribal-knowledge, on-process. No freeform tags. No multi-tagging within a single note.
rule.rationale: Closed taxonomy makes observations comparable across operators. Free-form coding leads to one-off categories nobody can roll up.

rule.statement: In listening sessions, all questions are open or behaviorally-grounded ("Walk me through what you did when…"). Reject sponsor-flavored questions ("Don't you think the new policy will…?").
rule.rationale: Leading questions raise socially-desirable response rates 30-50%. Behavioral grounding cuts retrospective bias.

rule.statement: The validated as-is model RECONCILES documented and observed processes. It must explicitly call out every divergence between official process and frontline reality, with the operator-level evidence supporting it. Do not silently overwrite the documented process with the observed one.
rule.rationale: Silent overwrites destroy the audit trail. Explicit reconciliation preserves "what we thought was happening" vs "what was actually happening" — essential for risk and governance review.

example.title: Two-operator validation matrix entry
example.code: Variant: EU customer onboarding (exception path: missing tax ID) Documented: SME process step 7 — "agent escalates to compliance queue" Observation A: Operator 1 (Berlin shift) bypasses compliance, calls customer directly to capture tax ID via email confirmation. 17 of 20 cases. Tagged: workaround. Observation B: Operator 2 (Munich shift) escalates 100% of cases to compliance. Tagged: on-process. Reconciliation: Documented process matches Operator 2; Operator 1 represents a workaround driven by SLA pressure (compliance queue avg response 4 days vs operator 1's 2-hour direct path).
example.note: Illustrates r1, r3, r5: two operators reveal the variance, taxonomy is closed, reconciliation is explicit.

example.title: Sponsor-flavored interview
example.code: BA: "The new policy is meant to be faster. Don't you find that the old escalation process was slowing things down?" Operator: "Yeah, I guess it was a bit slow." BA notes: "Operator confirms old process was slow."
example.note: Illustrates r4 violation. Question telegraphs the desired answer; operator's response carries no behavioral evidence.
